display_name: Transpacific Voyage — China to San Francisco

segments.travel_time: 15-25 days by sailing ship (1850s-1870s); 9-11 days by steamship (1880s onward)
segments.cost: $40-$50 steerage by sailing vessel (1850s-1860s); $30-$45 steerage by steamship (1880s-1900s). Most Chinese immigrants traveled under the credit-ticket system arranged by the Six Companies (Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association), repaying passage costs from wages after arrival.
segments.conditions: Steerage passengers were packed into the lower holds of ships, sleeping in tiered wooden bunks with no more than eighteen inches between them. The air below decks was thick with the smell of cooking rice, bilge water, and the sickness of men who had never seen the ocean before leaving their home villages in Guangdong Province. Sailing vessels pitched and rolled for weeks on end; men vomited into buckets passed hand to hand in the dark. The crew rarely allowed steerage passengers on deck. Food was rice and dried fish, served twice a day, and fresh water was rationed.

segments.to: San Francisco, California (Pacific Mail Steamship Company wharf)
segments.travel_time: 15-25 days by sailing ship; 9-10 days by steamship. Total voyage: 30-50 days by sail, 18-21 days by steam.
segments.cost: included in ticket; additional landing fees and Six Companies registration dues upon arrival
segments.conditions: As the ship entered the Golden Gate, Chinese passengers were separated from white passengers and held aboard for customs inspection. After 1882, the Chinese Exclusion Act required each arriving Chinese person to prove they belonged to an exempt class — merchant, student, diplomat, or returning laborer with a prior certificate of residence. Those who could not prove exempt status were detained, interrogated, and often deported. Before Angel Island opened in 1910, detainees were held in a shed on the Pacific Mail wharf or at the old wooden immigration station at the foot of Telegraph Hill.

who_used_it: Chinese men from Guangdong Province — primarily from the Sze Yup (four districts) and Sam Yup (three districts) regions — seeking work in California gold mines, railroad construction, agriculture, and service industries. An estimated 300,000 Chinese arrived in San Francisco between 1850 and 1882. After the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882), immigration dropped to a trickle of exempt classes and paper sons who purchased false identities to claim American birth. The Pacific Mail Steamship Company carried the majority of transpacific passengers from the 1860s through 1915.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} boarded a ship in {origin} bound for Gold Mountain, crossing 8,000 miles of open Pacific in steerage — weeks of darkness below decks, rice and salt fish twice a day, and the knowledge that the debt for the ticket would be waiting on the other side at {destination}.
source_references: Ronald Takaki, 'Strangers from a Different Shore: A History of Asian Americans' (Little, Brown and Company, 1989)
Sucheng Chan, 'Asian Americans: An Interpretive History' (Twayne Publishers, 1991)
Erika Lee, 'At America's Gates: Chinese Immigration During the Exclusion Era, 1882-1943' (University of North Carolina Press, 2003)
Andrew Gyory, 'Closing the Gate: Race, Politics, and the Chinese Exclusion Act' (University of North Carolina Press, 1998)
